comment_text.snp4.4:1.1: The sight of saint or holy person was believed to grant blessings (see AN 6.30, MN 81:6.5, Snp 2.4:10.2). As Bodhi remarks, aroga (“healthy”) refers to the “self”, which is frequently described as the subtle body that survives death (DN 1:2.38.3: rūpī attā hoti arogo). Here, however, what is “seen” is the soul as a physical body manifesting purity and wellness, as in the case of Māgaṇḍiya (MN 75:19.15).
comment_text.snp4.4:1.3: Here as in Snp 4.3, ñāṇa is used in the sense of “notion”. Bodhi, Norman, and Ñāṇadīpa all follow Niddesa in taking abhijāna in its usual sense of “understanding, direct knowing”. But here the secondary sense of “recall” fits better. Someone thinking about their vision of a holy man believes that there must be an observer of such purity, i.e. a self.
comment_text.snp4.4:1.4: Pacceti is a standard verb for someone who “believes” in a religious rite or idea.
comment_text.snp4.4:4.4: The monkey grabs the branches, not the trunk, as a metaphor for missing the essence. And to drive home the pun, religious orders are called “branches”.
comment_text.snp4.4:5.2: See uccāvacaṁ vā pana dassanāya gacchati at AN 6.30:2.2. In the current poem, it refers to someone who visits a variety of teachers or sects, as per Niddesa.
comment_text.snp4.4:5.3: Here the plural vedehi stands for the tevijjā.
comment_text.snp4.4:8.2: Note that samuggahīta is used here in the same sense as Snp 4.3:6.2 or Snp 2.12:11.1, i.e. the “adoption” of a theory or view.

root_text.snp4.4:2.4: Diṭṭhī hi naṁ pāva tathā vadānaṁ.
root_text.snp4.4:6.2: Yaṁ kiñci diṭṭhaṁ va sutaṁ mutaṁ vā;

translation_text.snp4.4:1.2: it is vision that grants a person purity.”
translation_text.snp4.4:1.3: Recalling this notion of the ultimate,
translation_text.snp4.4:1.4: they believe in the notion <j>that there is one who observes purity.
translation_text.snp4.4:2.1: If a person were granted purity through what is seen,
translation_text.snp4.4:2.2: or if by a notion they could give up suffering,
translation_text.snp4.4:2.3: then one with attachments is purified by another:
translation_text.snp4.4:2.4: their view betrays them as one who asserts thus.
translation_text.snp4.4:3.1: The brahmin speaks not of purity from another
translation_text.snp4.4:3.2: in terms of what has been seen, heard, or thought; <j>or by precepts or vows.
translation_text.snp4.4:3.3: They are unsullied in the midst of good and evil,
translation_text.snp4.4:3.4: letting go what was picked up, <j>without creating anything new here.
translation_text.snp4.4:4.1: Having let go the last they lay hold of the next;
translation_text.snp4.4:4.2: following impulse, they don’t pass the chain.
translation_text.snp4.4:4.3: They grab on and let loose like a monkey
translation_text.snp4.4:5.1: Having undertaken their own vows, a personage
translation_text.snp4.4:5.2: visits various teachers, <j>being attached to perception.
translation_text.snp4.4:5.3: One who knows, having comprehended the truth <j>through the knowledges,
translation_text.snp4.4:5.4: does not visit various teachers, being of vast wisdom.
translation_text.snp4.4:6.1: They are remote from all things
translation_text.snp4.4:6.4: how could anyone in this world judge them?
translation_text.snp4.4:7.1: They don’t make things up or promote them,
translation_text.snp4.4:7.2: or speak of the uttermost purity.
translation_text.snp4.4:7.3: After untying the tight knot of grasping
translation_text.snp4.4:7.4: they long for nothing in the world.
translation_text.snp4.4:8.1: The brahmin has stepped over the perimeter;
translation_text.snp4.4:8.2: knowing and seeing, they adopt nothing.
translation_text.snp4.4:8.3: Neither in love with passion <j>nor besotted by dispassion,
translation_text.snp4.4:8.4: there is nothing here they adopt as the ultimate.

variant_text.snp4.4:4.4: pamuñcaṁ gahāyaṁ → pamukhaṁ gahāya (bj, sya-all); pamuñcaṁ gahāya (pts-vp-pli1); pamuñca gahāya (mr)
variant_text.snp4.4:8.2: Ñatvā va disvā va → ñatvā ca disvā ca (si, mr)
